Homework 2

Part One: Analyzing the Weather

In this section, you will gain more practice working with public APIs, this time using a public weather API, WeatherAPI. The first thing you’ll need to access the API is an API key. You can sign up for a key here: https://www.weatherapi.com/signup.aspx

Exercise 1

Use the http://api.weatherapi.com/v1/current.json URL to access the API and obtain real-time weather data. Note that you will want to specify three query parameters, at least – key, which should be set to your individual API key, q, which should equal the city name of a specified location – for example q = "Isla Vista" – and aqi, which indicates whether you want to obtain air quality data ("yes" or "no").

Code 
library(httr)
key <- "b885a623f2b949429b9223628262804"
q <- "Isla Vista"
aqi <- "yes"

base_url <- "http://api.weatherapi.com/v1/current.json"
full_url <- paste0(base_url, "?key=", key, "&q=", URLencode(q), "&aqi=", aqi)

response <- GET(full_url)
data <- content(response, "parsed")

Obtain current real-time weather data for fifty cities. I have saved a data file containing the names of fifty cities to /data/cities.csv. This ensures that you are all working with the same locations (although your results will still differ, depending on when you obtain the data).

Code 
library(tidyverse)

cities_df <- read_csv("data/cities.csv")
city_names <- cities_df$names

raw_weather_data <- list()

for (city in city_names) {
  
  url <- paste0("http://api.weatherapi.com/v1/current.json?key=", key, 
                "&q=", URLencode(city), "&aqi=", aqi)
  response <- GET(url)
  
  if (status_code(response) == 200) {
    raw_weather_data[[city]] <- content(response, "parsed")
    } 
  else {
    print(concat("Failed to retrieve data for: ", city))
    }
  }

Exercise 2

Write code in R or Python (your choice) to extract and store the following data for each location:

  • City name

  • Country

  • Whether or not it is currently daytime there

  • Temperature (in Fahrenheit)

  • Humidity

  • Weather description (condition text; for example, “Mist”, “Clear”, etc.)

  • Wind speed (in miles per hour)

  • Precipitation (in millimeters)

  • US EPA air quality index (ranges from \(1\) to \(6\), representing the 6 categories of air quality: https://www.airnow.gov/aqi/aqi-basics/)

Code 
organized_data <- list()

for (city in names(raw_weather_data)) {
  data <- raw_weather_data[[city]]
  city_data <- tibble(
    City = data$location$name,
    Country = data$location$country,
    Daytime = as.logical(data$current$is_day), 
    Temp_F = data$current$temp_f,
    Humidity = data$current$humidity,
    Condition = data$current$condition$text,
    Wind_mph = data$current$wind_mph,
    Precip_mm = data$current$precip_mm,
    # I used AI here because I didn't realize that the us-epa-index needed additional formatting
    EPA_AQI = data$current$air_quality$`us-epa-index`
  )
  
  organized_data[[city]] <- city_data
}

# AI helped me with this part too, I was using rbind but it was not combining the data frames correctly
final_weather_df <- bind_rows(organized_data)

Exercise 3

Create a scatterplot of temperature vs. humidity. Add a linear regression line to the plot. What are the estimated intercept and slope values for this linear regression? Does there appear to be a significant relationship between temperature and humidity?

Code 
# Create the scatterplot with a linear regression line
ggplot(final_weather_df, aes(x = Temp_F, y = Humidity)) +
  geom_point(color = "blue", size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red", se = FALSE) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Temperature vs. Humidity",
       x = "Temperature (F)",
       y = "Humidity (%)")
Code 
temp_humid_model <- lm(Humidity ~ Temp_F, data = final_weather_df)
summary(temp_humid_model)

Call:
lm(formula = Humidity ~ Temp_F, data = final_weather_df)

Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
-57.71 -15.41  -0.65  15.55  31.70 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 63.72268   15.28046   4.170 0.000127 ***
Temp_F       0.05766    0.23133   0.249 0.804216    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 20.85 on 48 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.001293,  Adjusted R-squared:  -0.01951 
F-statistic: 0.06214 on 1 and 48 DF,  p-value: 0.8042

The estimated intercept is 75.3791, and the slope is -0.1038. For every 1 degree increase in temperature, humidity decreases by approximately 0.1038 percentage points. The regression line is almost flat and the p-value for the slope is very high, which indicates that the relationship is not significant.

Exercise 4

Create a bar chart of the EPA air quality index values. What does the distribution of air quality look like? Identify the location(s) with the best air quality and the worst air quality.

Code 
ggplot(final_weather_df, aes(x = factor(EPA_AQI))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "forestgreen", color = "black") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Distribution of EPA Air Quality Index",
       x = "EPA AQI (1 = Good, 6 = Hazardous)",
       y = "Number of Cities")
Code 
best_aqi_value <- min(final_weather_df$EPA_AQI, na.rm = TRUE)
worst_aqi_value <- max(final_weather_df$EPA_AQI, na.rm = TRUE)

cities_best_aqi <- final_weather_df %>% 
  filter(EPA_AQI == best_aqi_value) %>% 
  select(City, EPA_AQI)

cities_worst_aqi <- final_weather_df %>% 
  filter(EPA_AQI == worst_aqi_value) %>% 
  select(City, EPA_AQI)

cities_best_aqi
# A tibble: 22 × 2
   City       EPA_AQI
   <chr>        <int>
 1 Berlin           1
 2 Zunyi            1
 3 Rome             1
 4 Guayaquil        1
 5 Munich           1
 6 Casablanca       1
 7 Luanda           1
 8 Budapest         1
 9 Basra            1
10 Toronto          1
# ℹ 12 more rows
Code 
cities_worst_aqi
# A tibble: 1 × 2
  City   EPA_AQI
  <chr>    <int>
1 Riyadh       4

The distribution is right skewed, with most cities having good air quality and fewer cities having worse air quality. The location with the best air quality are Berlin, Zunyi, Rome, Guayaquil, Daegu, Munich, Casablanca, Luanda, Budapest, and Sydnet. The location with the worst air quality is Riyadh.

Exercise 5

Create a bar chart of the current weather description. Which conditions are the most common? Which are the least?

Code 
ggplot(final_weather_df, aes(y = forcats::fct_infreq(Condition))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "yellow", color = "black") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Current Weather Conditions",
       x = "Number of Cities",
       y = "Condition")
Code 
condition_counts <- final_weather_df %>%
  count(Condition, sort = TRUE)

The most common conditions are Clear and Partly cloudy. The least common conditions are Patchy light rain, Overcast, Light rain shower, and Light rain.

Part Two: Scraping Books

In this section, you’ll practice your web scraping skills by experimenting with a fictional online bookstore located at https://books.toscrape.com/. Use the tools that we demonstrate in class to do the following, in either R or Python (your choice):

Exercise 6

Scrape the first 20 results from this site. Create a data frame (or tibble) that stores the following for each book:

  • Title

  • Price (excluding tax)

  • Star rating

  • Whether the book is in stock

Code 
library(rvest)

url <- "https://books.toscrape.com/"
page <- read_html(url)

# The site stores each book inside an <article> tag with the class "product_pod"
articles <- page %>% html_elements("article.product_pod")

# Extract Titles 
titles <- articles %>% 
  html_element("h3 a") %>% 
  html_attr("title")

# Extract Prices
prices_raw <- articles %>% 
  html_element("p.price_color") %>% 
  html_text()
prices <- as.numeric(str_extract(prices_raw, "[0-9.]+"))

# Extract Star Ratings
stars_raw <- articles %>% 
  html_element("p.star-rating") %>% 
  html_attr("class") %>% 
  str_remove("star-rating ")

# convert text numbers to actual numbers
star_mapping <- c("One" = 1, "Two" = 2, "Three" = 3, "Four" = 4, "Five" = 5)
stars <- star_mapping[stars_raw]

# In Stock
stock_raw <- articles %>% 
  html_element("p.instock.availability") %>% 
  html_text()
in_stock <- str_detect(stock_raw, "In stock")

books_df <- tibble(
  Title = titles,
  Price = prices,
  Stars = stars,
  In_Stock = in_stock
)

Exercise 7

Create a histogram of prices for these 20 books. What is the average price?

Code 
ggplot(books_df, aes(x = Price)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = "blue", color = "white") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Book Prices",
       x = "Price",
       y = "Frequency")
Code 
average_price <- mean(books_df$Price)
average_price
[1] 38.0485

The average price is 38.0485 Euros.

Exercise 8

Create a bar chart of star rating for these 20 books. Find the book(s) with the highest and lowest star ratings.

Code 
ggplot(books_df, aes(x = factor(Stars, levels = 1:5))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "gold", color = "black") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Star Ratings",
       x = "Star Rating (1 to 5)",
       y = "Count of Books")
Code 
highest_rating_value <- max(books_df$Stars)
lowest_rating_value <- min(books_df$Stars)

best_books <- books_df %>% 
  filter(Stars == highest_rating_value)

worst_books <- books_df %>% 
  filter(Stars == lowest_rating_value)

best_books
# A tibble: 4 × 4
  Title                                                   Price Stars In_Stock
  <chr>                                                   <dbl> <dbl> <lgl>   
1 Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind                    54.2     5 TRUE    
2 Set Me Free                                              17.5     5 TRUE    
3 Scott Pilgrim's Precious Little Life (Scott Pilgrim #1)  52.3     5 TRUE    
4 Rip it Up and Start Again                                35.0     5 TRUE
Code 
worst_books
# A tibble: 6 × 4
  Title                                                 Price Stars In_Stock
  <chr>                                                 <dbl> <dbl> <lgl>   
1 Tipping the Velvet                                     53.7     1 TRUE    
2 Soumission                                             50.1     1 TRUE    
3 The Requiem Red                                        22.6     1 TRUE    
4 The Black Maria                                        52.2     1 TRUE    
5 Olio                                                   23.9     1 TRUE    
6 Mesaerion: The Best Science Fiction Stories 1800-1849  37.6     1 TRUE
